project.name: apache-thrift
project.description: A lightweight, language-independent software stack for point-to-point RPC implementation with code generation across 28+ programming languages

nodes.name: IDL Definition & Compilation
nodes.summary: Covers authoring Thrift IDL (.thrift) files that define services, types, and constants, then compiling them into target-language source code via the C++ compiler. This is the primary entry point for any Thrift-based project.

businessRules: IDL must be parsed into a well-formed AST before code generation
Each target language requires a dedicated generator registered in the generator registry
Backward compatibility can be audited via the audit tool before releasing schema changes
crossDomainInteractions: Produces generated source consumed by every language runtime (Transport & Networking, Protocol & Serialization, Service & Server Execution)
IDL test schemas drive Cross-Language Testing

nodes.name: Transport & Networking
nodes.summary: Handles the physical movement of bytes between Thrift clients and servers, abstracting over TCP sockets, framed streams, HTTP, TLS/SSL, WebSockets, and in-memory pipes. Each language library implements the same layered transport abstraction.

businessRules: Transports are composable decorators (e.g., framing wraps socket)
TLS configuration must be supplied before connection is opened
HTTP transport reuses the underlying TCP socket per request
crossDomainInteractions: Sits below the Protocol layer; Protocol reads/writes through a Transport
Server layer manages transport lifecycle (accept, read, write, close)

nodes.name: Protocol & Serialization
nodes.summary: Defines how Thrift data types are encoded and decoded on the wire. Provides Binary (compact binary), Compact (variable-length integer), JSON, and Simple JSON protocol implementations across all supported languages.

businessRules: Client and server must agree on the same protocol to communicate
Compact protocol reduces payload size via variable-length integers
JSON protocol enables human-readable debugging but is larger on the wire
crossDomainInteractions: Reads/writes through a Transport; sits between Transport and Processor layers
Generated stubs (from IDL Compilation) call protocol read/write methods

nodes.name: Service & Server Execution
nodes.summary: Covers the runtime execution model for Thrift services: processor dispatch from generated stubs, and server concurrency models ranging from single-threaded simple servers to non-blocking event-loop servers. This domain governs how an RPC call is accepted, dispatched, and responded to.

businessRules: Each server model requires at least a transport factory, protocol factory, and processor
TNonblockingServer requires libevent and uses a framed transport
TProcessor is generated per service by the IDL compiler
crossDomainInteractions: Depends on Transport & Networking to accept connections
Depends on Protocol & Serialization to decode requests and encode responses
Uses generated processors produced by IDL Compilation domain

nodes.name: Cross-Language Runtime Support
nodes.summary: Each supported language (Go, Java, Python, Ruby, C#/.NET, PHP, Erlang, Rust, Dart, Kotlin, TypeScript, and 17+ others) ships its own runtime library implementing the transport, protocol, and server abstractions. This domain governs the per-language implementations and their conformance to the Thrift specification.

businessRules: All language libraries must implement the same transport/protocol/server abstractions
Language-specific idioms are permitted within the common abstraction boundaries
New language libraries must pass cross-language integration tests before inclusion
crossDomainInteractions: Consumes generated stubs from IDL Compilation
Implements Transport & Networking and Protocol & Serialization contracts
Validated by Cross-Language Testing & Quality Assurance domain

nodes.name: Testing & Quality Assurance
nodes.summary: Encompasses cross-language integration tests driven by shared IDL schemas (ThriftTest.thrift, DebugProtoTest.thrift), CI/CD pipelines via GitHub Actions and AppVeyor, Docker-based build environments, and backward-compatibility auditing. Ensures correctness and interoperability across all supported languages.

businessRules: Every language pair must be tested for interoperability
Known failures are tracked and must not regress silently
TLS tests require pre-generated key materials checked into the repository
crossDomainInteractions: Exercises all runtime libraries (Cross-Language Runtime Support)
Uses IDL schemas produced in IDL Definition & Compilation domain
CI triggers on every PR targeting apache:master

nodes.name: Author a Thrift IDL File
nodes.summary: Developer writes a .thrift file defining namespaces, custom types (structs, enums, exceptions, unions), constants, and service methods that form the interface contract.

domainMeta.entryPoint: Developer creates or edits a .thrift file

nodes.name: Compile IDL to Target Language
nodes.summary: Invokes the Thrift compiler CLI on a .thrift file, which parses it into an AST, resolves includes and type references, selects the appropriate code generator, and emits target-language source files.

nodes.name: Audit IDL Backward Compatibility
nodes.summary: Uses the Thrift audit tool to compare two versions of a .thrift schema and detect breaking changes (removed fields, type changes, renamed services) before deployment.

nodes.name: Establish a Transport Connection
nodes.summary: Client instantiates and opens a transport (socket, TLS, or HTTP), optionally wrapping it with framing or buffering decorators, to create a reliable byte channel to the server.

nodes.name: Send and Receive Framed Data
nodes.summary: Writes a 4-byte length-prefixed frame containing an encoded RPC message over the transport, and reads the corresponding response frame from the server.

nodes.name: Configure TLS/SSL Transport
nodes.summary: Loads certificates and private keys, configures cipher suites and peer verification, and wraps a plain socket with TLS to produce an encrypted, authenticated channel.

nodes.name: Serialize an RPC Message
nodes.summary: Generated client stub calls protocol write methods to encode a method call (name, sequence ID, argument struct) using the configured protocol (Binary, Compact, or JSON) into the transport buffer.

nodes.name: Deserialize an RPC Response
nodes.summary: Client reads and decodes the protocol-encoded response message from the transport buffer, extracts the return value or exception struct, and returns the result to the caller.

domainMeta.entryPoint: Generated stub calls protocol.readMessageBegin() after transport flush

nodes.name: Select and Configure Wire Protocol
nodes.summary: Developer or operator chooses between Binary, Compact, or JSON protocols and supplies the chosen protocol factory to the server and client, ensuring both sides agree on encoding.

nodes.name: Start a Thrift Server
nodes.summary: Assembles a TServer with a processor, transport, protocol factories, and concurrency configuration, then calls serve() to begin accepting and handling client connections.

nodes.name: Handle an Inbound RPC Call
nodes.summary: Server transport accepts a connection, the processor reads and decodes the request using the protocol, dispatches to the service handler implementation, and writes the response back through the protocol and transport.

domainMeta.entryPoint: Inbound TCP connection accepted by server transport

nodes.name: Configure a Non-Blocking Server
nodes.summary: Sets up TNonblockingServer (C++) with libevent, framed transport, thread-pool task queue, and I/O threads to achieve high-throughput event-driven request handling.

nodes.name: Implement a Thrift Service in Go
nodes.summary: Uses the Go runtime library to create a client or server: instantiates socket/framed transports, binary or compact protocol, and runs a TSimpleServer or goroutine-based server backed by generated Go stubs.

domainMeta.entryPoint: go get and import thrift package in Go project

nodes.name: Implement a Thrift Service in Java
nodes.summary: Uses the Java runtime library (Maven/Gradle dependency) with generated Java stubs to build clients and servers, leveraging TThreadPoolServer or TNonblockingServer for production use.

domainMeta.entryPoint: Add libthrift dependency to Maven/Gradle project

nodes.name: Implement a Thrift Service in Python
nodes.summary: Uses the Python runtime library (pip install thrift) with generated Python stubs. Supports pure-Python and C-accelerated protocol implementations with TSimpleServer or TThreadedServer.

domainMeta.entryPoint: pip install thrift; import thrift in Python project

nodes.name: Implement a Thrift Service in .NET
nodes.summary: Uses the .NET Standard runtime library with async transport and protocol implementations. Generated C# stubs integrate with the async server model for modern .NET applications.

nodes.name: Implement a Thrift Service in Node.js/TypeScript
nodes.summary: Uses the Node.js or TypeScript runtime library with WebSocket or HTTP transport support. Generated TypeScript stubs provide type-safe clients and server skeletons.

domainMeta.entryPoint: npm install thrift; require('thrift') in Node.js project

nodes.name: Run Cross-Language Integration Tests
nodes.summary: Executes the cross-language test suite using ThriftTest.thrift and DebugProtoTest.thrift: starts server processes in each language and runs client tests against them for all protocol/transport combinations.

nodes.name: CI Pipeline Validation
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions and AppVeyor workflows trigger on every pull request to build all language libraries, run unit and integration tests in Docker containers, and report results before merge.

nodes.name: Track and Manage Known Test Failures
nodes.summary: Maintains a known-failures list for cross-language test combinations that are not yet passing, preventing noise in CI while still tracking open issues for eventual resolution.

domainMeta.entryPoint: Update known_failures file when a test combination is broken

nodes.name: Define Namespaces
nodes.summary: Declare per-language namespaces (e.g., namespace cpp tutorial, namespace java org.example) to control generated package/module names in each target language.

nodes.name: Declare Types (Structs, Enums, Exceptions)
nodes.summary: Define the data model using struct, enum, exception, and union keywords. Fields have integer IDs (for wire compatibility) and optional/required qualifiers.

nodes.name: Define Service Methods
nodes.summary: Declare service interfaces with typed method signatures, including oneway methods (fire-and-forget), throws clauses for exception types, and return types.

nodes.name: Define Constants
nodes.summary: Use the const keyword to declare typed constants that are emitted as language-native constants in generated code, enabling shared configuration values across clients and servers.

nodes.name: Parse CLI Arguments
nodes.summary: main.cc parses the --gen <lang> flag, include paths (-I), output directory (-o), and other compiler options from the command line.

nodes.name: Lex and Parse IDL with Flex/Bison
nodes.summary: The Flex lexer tokenises the .thrift source, and the Bison parser constructs an AST rooted at t_program, resolving includes recursively.

nodes.name: Validate and Resolve AST
nodes.summary: Post-parse validation resolves type references across includes, checks for duplicate field IDs, validates identifier uniqueness, and applies default values.

nodes.name: Lookup Code Generator
nodes.summary: The compiler queries the generator registry (t_generator_registry) with the target language name to instantiate the appropriate t_generator subclass.

nodes.name: Emit Target-Language Source Files
nodes.summary: The selected generator traverses the AST and writes out language-specific source files: type definitions, service clients, processor stubs, and constants.

nodes.name: Load Old and New Schema ASTs
nodes.summary: The audit tool parses both the old and new .thrift files into separate ASTs for structural comparison.

nodes.name: Compare Type and Field Changes
nodes.summary: Detects removed fields, changed field types, new required fields without defaults, and renamed service methods that would break wire compatibility.

nodes.name: Report Breaking Changes
nodes.summary: Outputs a human-readable report of detected backward-incompatible changes, allowing developers to make informed decisions before deploying schema updates.

nodes.name: Instantiate Socket Transport
nodes.summary: Create a TSocket (or TSSLSocket) with host and port, which wraps a POSIX or OS socket file descriptor.

nodes.name: Wrap Transport with Decorators
nodes.summary: Optionally wrap the base socket with TBufferedTransport (in-memory buffering) and/or TFramedTransport (length-prefix framing) using the decorator pattern.

nodes.name: Open the Connection
nodes.summary: Call transport.open() which performs the TCP connect (or TLS handshake if using TSSLSocket), making the channel ready for protocol read/write operations.

nodes.name: Write 4-Byte Frame Length Header
nodes.summary: TFramedTransport prepends the serialized message size as a big-endian 32-bit integer before writing the message body to the underlying transport.

nodes.name: Flush Message to Wire
nodes.summary: The transport flushes its write buffer, pushing the complete framed message through the socket to the remote endpoint.

nodes.name: Read Response Frame
nodes.summary: Client reads the 4-byte length header, then reads exactly that many bytes to obtain the complete response frame for protocol decoding.

nodes.name: Load Certificates and Private Key
nodes.summary: Configure TSSLSocket/TSSLServerSocket with paths to the CA certificate bundle, entity certificate, and private key file for mutual or server-only TLS authentication.

nodes.name: Set Cipher Suite and Verification Policy
nodes.summary: Select allowed cipher suites, minimum TLS protocol version, and peer verification mode (none, optional, or require) to enforce the desired security posture.

nodes.name: Perform TLS Handshake
nodes.summary: On open(), the TSSLSocket executes the OpenSSL handshake, exchanging certificates and negotiating session keys before any application data is sent.

nodes.name: Write Message Header
nodes.summary: Protocol writes the message header containing the method name, message type (call/oneway), and sequence ID to identify the RPC call on the wire.

nodes.name: Write Argument Struct
nodes.summary: The generated client stub calls the auto-generated argument struct's write() method, which encodes each field with its type tag and field ID using the protocol.

nodes.name: Flush Protocol Buffer
nodes.summary: Call protocol.getTransport().flush() to push the fully-encoded message through the transport stack to the remote endpoint.

nodes.name: Read Message Header
nodes.summary: Protocol reads the response message header, verifying the sequence ID and message type (reply vs exception) to detect protocol errors or mismatches.

nodes.name: Read Result Struct
nodes.summary: The generated result struct's read() method decodes each field by type tag and field ID, populating the return value or exception fields.

nodes.name: Throw Exception or Return Value
nodes.summary: If the result struct contains an exception field, it is thrown; otherwise the return value field is extracted and returned to the calling application code.

nodes.name: Instantiate Protocol Factory
nodes.summary: Instantiate TBinaryProtocolFactory, TCompactProtocolFactory, or TJSONProtocolFactory to be passed to both client and server during setup.

nodes.name: Supply Protocol Factory to Server
nodes.summary: Pass the protocol factory to the TServer constructor so every accepted connection uses the chosen protocol for encoding and decoding messages.

nodes.name: Supply Protocol to Client
nodes.summary: Wrap the client transport with the chosen protocol instance and pass to the generated client class constructor for outbound call encoding.

nodes.name: Assemble Server Components
nodes.summary: Instantiate a server socket (TServerSocket), transport factory (TFramedTransportFactory), protocol factory, and the service processor (generated TProcessor subclass).

nodes.name: Configure Concurrency Model
nodes.summary: Choose between TSimpleServer (single-threaded), TThreadedServer (thread-per-connection), TThreadPoolServer (bounded pool), or TNonblockingServer (event-loop) based on throughput requirements.

nodes.name: Call serve() to Accept Connections
nodes.summary: Invoke server.serve() which blocks the calling thread (or enters an event loop) and begins accepting client connections, dispatching them to the processor.

nodes.name: Accept Inbound Connection
nodes.summary: The server transport accepts a new TCP connection, creating a per-connection client transport and handing it to the server's connection handler.

nodes.name: Read and Decode Request
nodes.summary: The processor reads the message header and argument struct from the protocol, identifying which service method was called and its input arguments.

nodes.name: Dispatch to Service Handler
nodes.summary: The generated processor maps the method name to the corresponding handler implementation method and invokes it with the decoded arguments.

nodes.name: Encode and Send Response
nodes.summary: The processor writes the result struct (or exception) back through the protocol and flushes the transport to deliver the response to the client.

nodes.name: Initialize libevent Event Base
nodes.summary: TNonblockingServer creates a libevent event_base to drive the I/O event loop, registering the server socket for accept events.

nodes.name: Configure Thread Manager
nodes.summary: Set up a PosixThreadFactory and ThreadManager with the desired worker thread count to handle CPU-bound request processing off the I/O event threads.

nodes.name: Start I/O Threads
nodes.summary: TNonblockingServer spawns one or more I/O threads, each running its own libevent loop, to multiplex many concurrent connections without blocking.

nodes.name: Add Go Module Dependency
nodes.summary: Add github.com/apache/thrift to go.mod and run go get to fetch the Go runtime library package.

nodes.name: Generate Go Stubs
nodes.summary: Run thrift --gen go on the .thrift file to produce Go client, processor, and type files in the output directory.

nodes.name: Create Go Client or Server
nodes.summary: Instantiate thrift.NewTSocket, wrap with thrift.NewTFramedTransport, create a thrift.TBinaryProtocol, and use generated client/server structs to make calls or serve requests.

nodes.name: Add Maven/Gradle Dependency
nodes.summary: Add the org.apache.thrift:libthrift artifact to pom.xml or build.gradle to include the Java runtime library.

nodes.name: Generate Java Stubs
nodes.summary: Run thrift --gen java on the .thrift file. The Java generator emits Iface and Client classes per service, plus Java type classes for structs and enums.

nodes.name: Build and Start Java Server
nodes.summary: Implement the service Iface, construct a TServer with transport/protocol factories and the generated processor, and call server.serve() to begin handling requests.

nodes.name: Install Python Thrift Package
nodes.summary: Run pip install thrift to install the Python runtime library, which includes both the pure-Python and optional C-accelerated protocol implementations.

nodes.name: Generate Python Stubs
nodes.summary: Run thrift --gen py on the .thrift file to produce Python modules for types, service clients, and server-side Iface/Processor classes.

nodes.name: Run Python Server or Client
nodes.summary: Instantiate TSocket, optionally wrap with TBufferedTransport/TFramedTransport, create a TBinaryProtocol, and use the generated client or TServer/TProcessor to handle calls.

nodes.name: Add NuGet Package Reference
nodes.summary: Reference the Apache.Thrift NuGet package in the .csproj file to bring in the .NET Standard runtime library with async transport and protocol support.

nodes.name: Generate C# Stubs
nodes.summary: Run thrift --gen netstd on the .thrift file to produce async C# interfaces, client classes, and processor stubs targeting .NET Standard.

nodes.name: Host Async .NET Server
nodes.summary: Implement the async service interface, create a TServer with async transport/protocol factories, and use async/await patterns throughout the request handling pipeline.

nodes.name: Install npm Package
nodes.summary: Run npm install thrift to install the Node.js runtime library, or npm install @creditkarma/thrift-server-core for the TypeScript variant.

nodes.name: Generate Node.js/TypeScript Stubs
nodes.summary: Run thrift --gen js:node or --gen ts on the .thrift file to produce JavaScript or TypeScript client and processor modules.

nodes.name: Connect via WebSocket or HTTP
nodes.summary: Use the Node.js transport layer to create a WebSocket or HTTP-based connection, enabling browser or server-side clients to communicate with a Thrift service.

nodes.name: Define Test IDL Schemas
nodes.summary: ThriftTest.thrift and DebugProtoTest.thrift define a comprehensive set of service methods and types used to exercise all protocol and transport combinations across languages.

nodes.name: Generate Test Stubs for All Languages
nodes.summary: The build system runs the Thrift compiler on test IDL files for each language to produce test client and server stubs used in integration tests.

nodes.name: Start Language-Specific Test Servers
nodes.summary: The test runner launches test server processes (one per language) on configured ports and protocol/transport combinations, ready to accept integration test clients.

nodes.name: Run Cross-Language Client Assertions
nodes.summary: Test clients in each language connect to servers in every other language and execute service method calls, asserting correct return values and exception propagation.

nodes.name: Report Test Results
nodes.summary: The test runner collects pass/fail results for each language-pair and protocol combination, filters known failures, and produces a summary report.

nodes.name: Trigger CI on Pull Request
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions workflows are triggered by pull_request events targeting apache:master, initiating parallel build and test jobs across language matrix.

nodes.name: Build in Docker Environment
nodes.summary: Each CI job pulls a language-specific Docker image with compiler toolchains, builds the Thrift compiler and runtime library, and runs unit tests in isolation.

nodes.name: Run Integration Tests in CI
nodes.summary: After successful unit test builds, CI runs the cross-language integration test suite and fails the pipeline if any unexpected test regressions are detected.

nodes.name: Report CI Status to PR
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions posts build/test status checks back to the pull request, gating merge until all required checks pass.

nodes.name: Identify Failing Language/Protocol Combination
nodes.summary: When a cross-language test combination consistently fails, it is identified by language pair, protocol, and transport combination for tracking.

nodes.name: Record in Known Failures File
nodes.summary: The failing combination is added to the known_failures configuration so CI skips asserting it as a regression while still running the test.

nodes.name: File JIRA Issue for Resolution
nodes.summary: A JIRA ticket in the THRIFT project is filed to track the known failure, linking it to the known_failures entry so it can be resolved and removed in a future PR.

edges.description: Generated stubs use transport APIs to send/receive encoded RPC messages
Generated read()/write() methods call protocol encode/decode primitives
Generated TProcessor subclasses are the primary input to server execution
Each language runtime consumes stubs generated from IDL for client/server construction
ThriftTest.thrift and DebugProtoTest.thrift are the IDL schemas driving all integration tests
Protocol layer reads/writes bytes through the transport abstraction
Server processor decodes requests and encodes responses through the protocol layer
Each language runtime implements the transport abstraction for its ecosystem
Each language runtime implements Binary, Compact, and JSON protocols
Each language runtime provides server implementations (simple, threaded, nonblocking)
Integration tests exercise all language runtime implementations for cross-language interoperability
